document.source_title: WHO prequalifies first-ever malaria treatment for newborns and infants, adds new diagnostic tests

claims.claim_text: WHO prequalifies the first treatment developed specifically for newborns and young infants weighing between two and five kilograms.
claims.evidence_text: The prequalification designation indicates that the medicine meets international standards of quality, safety and efficacy.

claims.claim_text: WHO prequalified three new rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) designed to address emerging diagnostic challenges for malaria.
claims.evidence_text: WHO also prequalified three new rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) designed to address emerging diagnostic challenges for malaria.

entities.name: World Health Organization
entities.description: The UN agency for health that connects nations, partners and people on the front lines.
entities.evidence_text: Dedicated to the well-being of all people and guided by science, the World Health Organization leads and champions global efforts.

entities.name: malaria
entities.description: A disease caused by parasites that are transmitted to people through the bites of infected mosquitoes.
entities.evidence_text: For centuries, malaria has stolen children from their parents, and health, wealth and hope from communities.

events.event: WHO prequalifies new rapid diagnostic tests for malaria.
events.evidence_text: On 14 April 2026, WHO also prequalified three new rapid diagnostic tests (RDTs) designed to address emerging diagnostic challenges for malaria.
Ahead of World Malaria Day on 25 April, the World Health Organization (WHO) has announced a significant step forward in the fight against malaria.

metrics.name: estimated malaria cases
metrics.evidence_text: According to the World malaria report 2025, there were an estimated 282 million cases and 610 000 deaths in 2024.

metrics.name: estimated malaria deaths
metrics.evidence_text: According to the World malaria report 2025, there were an estimated 282 million cases and 610 000 deaths in 2024.

metrics.name: lives saved since 2000
metrics.evidence_text: Substantial progress has been made, with an estimated 2.3 billion malaria infections prevented and 14 million lives saved worldwide since 2000.

citations.title: WHO prequalifies first-ever malaria treatment for newborns and infants, adds new diagnostic tests

source_credibility.reason: The source is the World Health Organization, a recognized authority in global health.
